expanded_description: A comprehensive guide to net ionic equations, exploring how to identify and remove spectator ions to focus on the actual chemical change occurring in aqueous reactions, enabling clearer understanding of reaction mechanisms and stoichiometry.

core_concept.principle: Net ionic equations show only the species that actually participate in a chemical reaction by removing spectator ions that remain unchanged in solution.
core_concept.explanation: In aqueous reactions, many ions are present but don't actually participate in the chemical change—these are spectator ions. By writing complete ionic equations (showing all dissolved ions) and then cancelling spectator ions, we obtain net ionic equations that reveal the true chemical change. This simplifies understanding of reaction mechanisms, helps predict products, and is essential for understanding precipitation, acid-base, and redox reactions. Net ionic equations are more concise and show the essential chemistry occurring.

image.alt: Diagram showing molecular, complete ionic, and net ionic equations side by side
image.caption: Net ionic equations: remove spectator ions to show only reacting species.

sections.introduction: When ionic compounds dissolve in water, they dissociate into their constituent ions. In many reactions, some of these ions don't actually participate in the chemical change—they simply 'watch' the reaction occur. These are called spectator ions, and identifying them is the key to writing net ionic equations.

key_concept.definition: Spectator ions are ions that appear unchanged on both sides of a complete ionic equation; they are present in solution but do not participate in the actual chemical reaction.
key_concept.context: Spectator ions remain dissolved in solution throughout the reaction. They can be cancelled out when writing net ionic equations because they don't undergo any chemical change. Identifying spectator ions requires comparing ions on reactant and product sides.

image.alt: Diagram showing spectator ions remaining in solution while other ions react
image.caption: Spectator ions: present but unchanged; cancelled in net ionic equation.

principles.statement: Spectator ions are identified by comparing the complete ionic equation: ions that appear identically on both reactant and product sides are spectators.
principles.application: $\text{Ag}^+(aq) + \text{NO}_3^-(aq) + \text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s) + \text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{NO}_3^-(aq)$. $\text{Na}^+$ and $\text{NO}_3^-$ appear on both sides unchanged = spectator ions.
principles.analogy: Like bystanders at an accident: present at the scene but not involved. Spectator ions present in solution but don't react.

image.alt: Step-by-step identification of spectator ions in complete ionic equation
image.caption: Spectator identification: compare both sides, cancel ions that appear unchanged.

principles.statement: Removing spectator ions simplifies equations, reveals the actual chemical change, and shows that many different molecular reactions share the same net ionic equation.
principles.application: Multiple reactions can have same net ionic equation: $\text{AgNO}_3 + \text{NaCl}$, $\text{AgNO}_3 + \text{KCl}$, $\text{AgC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2 + \text{NaCl}$ all have net ionic: $\text{Ag}^+ + \text{Cl}^- \rightarrow \text{AgCl}$.
principles.example: All soluble silver salt + soluble chloride reactions: $\text{Ag}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s)$. Same net ionic regardless of which silver salt or chloride salt used.
principles.analogy: Like removing background noise to hear the actual conversation. Spectator ions are 'noise'; net ionic shows the 'conversation' (actual reaction).

image.alt: Comparison showing clarity of net ionic vs. complete ionic equations
image.caption: Net ionic clarity: removes spectators to show actual chemical change.

principles.principle: Spectator Ions in Different Reaction Types
principles.statement: Different reaction types have different spectator ion patterns: precipitation (counter-ions are spectators), neutralization (salt ions are spectators), single displacement (unchanged ion is spectator).
principles.application: Precipitation: $\text{AgNO}_3 + \text{NaCl}$ → spectators $\text{Na}^+$, $\text{NO}_3^-$. Neutralization: $\text{HCl} + \text{NaOH}$ → spectators $\text{Na}^+$, $\text{Cl}^-$. Single displacement: $\text{Zn} + \text{CuSO}_4$ → spectator $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$.
principles.example: Precipitation: $\text{BaCl}_2 + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$ → spectators $\text{Na}^+$, $\text{Cl}^-$. Neutralization: $\text{HNO}_3 + \text{KOH}$ → spectators $\text{K}^+$, $\text{NO}_3^-$. Pattern recognition speeds identification.

image.alt: Chart showing spectator ion patterns for different reaction types
image.caption: Spectator patterns: precipitation (counter-ions), neutralization (salt ions), displacement (unchanged ion).

components.interpretation: Compare complete ionic equation. Ions identical on both sides (same formula, charge, state) = spectators. Cancel them to get net ionic.

worked_examples.title: Identifying Spectator Ions
worked_examples.problem: Identify the spectator ions in the reaction between potassium carbonate and calcium chloride: $\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3(aq) + \text{CaCl}_2(aq) \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3(s) + 2\text{KCl}(aq)$.

step_by_step.explanation: Write the complete ionic equation (dissociate all aqueous ionic compounds).
step_by_step.calculation: $2\text{K}^+(aq) + \text{CO}_3^{2-}(aq) + \text{Ca}^{2+}(aq) + 2\text{Cl}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3(s) + 2\text{K}^+(aq) + 2\text{Cl}^-(aq)$

step_by_step.explanation: Compare ions on reactant and product sides.
step_by_step.calculation: Reactants: $2\text{K}^+$, $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$, $\text{Ca}^{2+}$, $2\text{Cl}^-$. Products: $\text{CaCO}_3(s)$, $2\text{K}^+$, $2\text{Cl}^-$

step_by_step.explanation: Identify ions that appear unchanged on both sides.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{K}^+(aq)$: appears on both sides ✓. $\text{Cl}^-(aq)$: appears on both sides ✓. $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ and $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$: form precipitate, not spectators.
Spectator ions: $\text{K}^+(aq)$ and $\text{Cl}^-(aq)$

worked_examples.final_answer: Spectator ions: $\text{K}^+(aq)$ and $\text{Cl}^-(aq)$. These ions appear unchanged on both sides of the complete ionic equation. The actual reaction is between $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ and $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$ to form $\text{CaCO}_3$ precipitate.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Identifying spectator ions by comparing complete ionic equation reactant and product sides

worked_examples.title: Spectator Ions in Neutralization
worked_examples.problem: Identify spectator ions in the neutralization reaction: $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4(aq) + 2\text{NaOH}(aq) \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4(aq) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$.

step_by_step.calculation: $2\text{H}^+(aq) + \text{SO}_4^{2-}(aq) + 2\text{Na}^+(aq) + 2\text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow 2\text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{SO}_4^{2-}(aq) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$
Reactants: $2\text{H}^+$, $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$, $2\text{Na}^+$, $2\text{OH}^-$. Products: $2\text{Na}^+$, $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$, $2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$
$\text{Na}^+(aq)$: appears on both sides ✓. $\text{SO}_4^{2-}(aq)$: appears on both sides ✓. $\text{H}^+$ and $\text{OH}^-$ form water, not spectators.
Spectator ions: $\text{Na}^+(aq)$ and $\text{SO}_4^{2-}(aq)$

worked_examples.final_answer: Spectator ions: $\text{Na}^+(aq)$ and $\text{SO}_4^{2-}(aq)$. These are the salt ions that remain in solution. The actual reaction is $\text{H}^+(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$, which is the net ionic for ALL strong acid-strong base neutralizations.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Identifying spectator ions in neutralization reactions (salt ions are spectators)

sections.introduction: Before writing net ionic equations, you must first write complete ionic equations that show all dissolved ionic compounds as dissociated ions. This intermediate step is essential for correctly identifying spectator ions.

key_concept.definition: A complete ionic equation shows all soluble ionic compounds dissociated into their constituent ions, while insoluble compounds, liquids, gases, and weak electrolytes remain written as complete formulas.
key_concept.context: Complete ionic equations reveal all species present in solution. Soluble ionic compounds (strong electrolytes) dissociate completely. Insoluble compounds (precipitates), molecular compounds, weak acids/bases, liquids, and gases do not dissociate and remain as formulas.

image.alt: Step-by-step guide for writing complete ionic equations
image.caption: Complete ionic: dissociate soluble ionic compounds; keep solids/liquids/gases as formulas.

classifications.value: Write as separate ions in complete ionic equations
classifications.behavior: These compounds completely dissociate into ions in aqueous solution. Write each ion separately with correct charge and $(aq)$ state. Include coefficients from balanced equation.

examples.explanation: $\text{NaCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq)$. In complete ionic equation, write as separate ions. $\text{BaCl}_2(aq) \rightarrow \text{Ba}^{2+}(aq) + 2\text{Cl}^-(aq)$. Include coefficient for multiple ions.

image.alt: Diagram showing ionic compound dissociating into ions
image.caption: Strong electrolytes: dissociate completely into ions in solution.

examples.explanation: $\text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{H}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq)$. Strong acids dissociate completely. $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4(aq) \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+(aq) + \text{SO}_4^{2-}(aq)$. Include all $\text{H}^+$ ions.

image.alt: Strong acid dissociating into H+ and anion
image.caption: Strong acids: dissociate completely, write as $\text{H}^+$ + anion.

classifications.value: Write as complete formulas in complete ionic equations
classifications.behavior: These compounds do not dissociate significantly in water. Write as complete molecular formulas with $(aq)$ state. Do not separate into ions.

examples.explanation: $\text{HC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2(aq)$ (acetic acid) remains written as $\text{HC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2(aq)$, not as $\text{H}^+ + \text{C}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2^-$. Only ~1% dissociated.

image.alt: Weak acid remaining mostly as molecules
image.caption: Weak acids: remain mostly molecular, write as complete formula.

examples.explanation: $\text{NH}_3(aq)$ remains written as $\text{NH}_3(aq)$, not as ions. Reacts with water but doesn't dissociate like strong bases.

image.alt: Ammonia remaining as molecules in solution
image.caption: Weak bases like $\text{NH}_3$: remain molecular in complete ionic equations.

classifications.value: Write as complete formulas in complete ionic equations
classifications.behavior: These substances do not form ions in solution. Write as complete molecular formulas with appropriate state symbols $(l)$, $(g)$, or $(s)$.

examples.explanation: $\text{AgCl}(s)$ remains written as $\text{AgCl}(s)$, not as ions. Precipitates are insoluble and don't dissociate.

image.alt: Precipitate remaining as solid formula
image.caption: Precipitates: insoluble, write as complete formula with $(s)$ state.

examples.explanation: $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$ remains written as $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$, not as ions. Water is a liquid, very weak electrolyte.

image.alt: Water remaining as molecular formula
image.caption: Water: liquid, write as $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$ in complete ionic equations.

principles.statement: Use solubility rules to determine if ionic compound is soluble (dissociate into ions) or insoluble (keep as formula).
principles.application: $\text{NaCl}$: soluble (Group 1) → write as $\text{Na}^+ + \text{Cl}^-$. $\text{AgCl}$: insoluble (Ag exception) → write as $\text{AgCl}(s)$. $\text{BaSO}_4$: insoluble (Ba exception) → write as $\text{BaSO}_4(s)$.
principles.example: Complete ionic: $\text{Ag}^+(aq) + \text{NO}_3^-(aq) + \text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s) + \text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{NO}_3^-(aq)$. $\text{AgCl}$ insoluble → keep as formula.

image.alt: Flowchart showing solubility rules determining dissociation
image.caption: Solubility rules: soluble → ions; insoluble → formula.

principles.statement: State symbols indicate whether to dissociate: $(aq)$ ionic compounds dissociate; $(s)$, $(l)$, $(g)$ do not dissociate.
principles.application: $\text{NaCl}(aq)$ → dissociate to $\text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq)$. $\text{NaCl}(s)$ → keep as $\text{NaCl}(s)$ (solid doesn't dissociate). $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$ → keep as $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$.
principles.example: $\text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{H}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq)$ (aqueous, dissociates). $\text{HCl}(g) \rightarrow$ keep as $\text{HCl}(g)$ (gas, doesn't dissociate). State matters!

image.alt: State symbols and their dissociation implications
image.caption: State symbols: $(aq)$ ionic = dissociate; $(s)$, $(l)$, $(g)$ = keep as formula.

principles.statement: When dissociating compounds with coefficients, multiply the coefficient by each ion's subscript to get total ion count.

image.alt: Diagram showing how coefficients multiply ion counts
image.caption: Coefficients: multiply by subscripts to get total ion count.

components.interpretation: Dissociate strong electrolytes (soluble ionic, strong acids/bases). Keep weak/non-electrolytes as formulas. Include correct charges and state symbols.

worked_examples.title: Writing Complete Ionic Equation
worked_examples.problem: Write the complete ionic equation for: $\text{Pb}(\text{NO}_3)_2(aq) + 2\text{KI}(aq) \rightarrow \text{PbI}_2(s) + 2\text{KNO}_3(aq)$.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify which compounds dissociate (soluble ionic) and which don't (precipitate).
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Pb}(\text{NO}_3)_2(aq)$: soluble (nitrates always soluble) → dissociate. $\text{KI}(aq)$: soluble (Group 1) → dissociate. $\text{PbI}_2(s)$: insoluble precipitate → keep as formula. $\text{KNO}_3(aq)$: soluble → dissociate.

step_by_step.explanation: Dissociate soluble compounds into ions with correct charges and coefficients.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Pb}(\text{NO}_3)_2(aq) \rightarrow \text{Pb}^{2+}(aq) + 2\text{NO}_3^-(aq)$. $2\text{KI}(aq) \rightarrow 2\text{K}^+(aq) + 2\text{I}^-(aq)$. $2\text{KNO}_3(aq) \rightarrow 2\text{K}^+(aq) + 2\text{NO}_3^-(aq)$.

step_by_step.explanation: Write complete ionic equation with all species.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Pb}^{2+}(aq) + 2\text{NO}_3^-(aq) + 2\text{K}^+(aq) + 2\text{I}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{PbI}_2(s) + 2\text{K}^+(aq) + 2\text{NO}_3^-(aq)$
Atoms: 1 Pb, 2 N, 6 O, 2 K, 2 I both sides ✓. Charge: left (+2) + 2(-1) + 2(+1) + 2(-1) = 0; right 0 (neutral solid) + 2(+1) + 2(-1) = 0 ✓.

worked_examples.final_answer: Complete ionic: $\text{Pb}^{2+}(aq) + 2\text{NO}_3^-(aq) + 2\text{K}^+(aq) + 2\text{I}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{PbI}_2(s) + 2\text{K}^+(aq) + 2\text{NO}_3^-(aq)$. Soluble compounds dissociated; precipitate kept as formula. Atoms and charges balanced.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Writing complete ionic equation by dissociating soluble compounds and keeping precipitates as formulas

worked_examples.title: Complete Ionic with Weak Acid
worked_examples.problem: Write the complete ionic equation for: $\text{NaOH}(aq) + \text{HC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2(aq) \rightarrow \text{NaC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$.

step_by_step.calculation: $\text{NaOH}(aq)$: strong base → dissociate. $\text{HC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2(aq)$: weak acid (acetic acid) → keep as formula. $\text{NaC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2(aq)$: soluble ionic → dissociate. $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$: liquid → keep as formula.

$\text{NaOH}(aq) \rightarrow \text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq)$. $\text{HC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2(aq)$ → keep as $\text{HC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2(aq)$. $\text{NaC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2(aq) \rightarrow \text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{C}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2^-(aq)$.
$\text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq) + \text{HC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2(aq) \rightarrow \text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{C}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2^-(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$
Atoms: 1 Na, 1 O, 1 H (from $\text{OH}^-$) + 4 H (from acid) = 5 H, 2 C, 3 O (from acid) + 1 O (from $\text{OH}^-$) = 4 O both sides ✓. Charge: left (+1) + (-1) + 0 = 0; right (+1) + (-1) + 0 = 0 ✓.

worked_examples.final_answer: Complete ionic: $\text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq) + \text{HC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2(aq) \rightarrow \text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{C}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2^-(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. Weak acid kept as formula; strong base and soluble salt dissociated. Note: weak acids do NOT dissociate in complete ionic equations.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Writing complete ionic equation with weak electrolytes (keep weak acids as formulas)

sections.introduction: Once you have the complete ionic equation, writing the net ionic equation is straightforward: cancel spectator ions that appear unchanged on both sides. The remaining species are the ones actually participating in the chemical change.

key_concept.definition: A net ionic equation shows only the species that actually participate in a chemical reaction, with spectator ions removed. It represents the essential chemical change occurring in the reaction.
key_concept.context: Net ionic equations are more concise than molecular or complete ionic equations. They reveal the actual chemistry occurring and show that many different molecular reactions share the same net ionic equation. Essential for understanding precipitation, acid-base, and redox reactions.

image.alt: Step-by-step process for writing net ionic equations from complete ionic
image.caption: Net ionic steps: write complete ionic → cancel spectators → write net ionic → verify balance.

principles.statement: Cancel spectator ions by crossing out identical ions (same formula, charge, state, and coefficient) on both sides of the complete ionic equation.
principles.example: After canceling: $\text{Ag}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s)$. Only reacting species remain. Spectators removed.
principles.analogy: Like editing a document: remove unnecessary words (spectators) to reveal the core message (actual reaction).

image.alt: Diagram showing spectator ions crossed out from complete ionic equation
image.caption: Cancel spectators: cross out identical ions on both sides.

principles.statement: After canceling spectators, verify the net ionic equation is balanced for both atoms and charge.
principles.application: Net ionic: $\text{Ba}^{2+}(aq) + \text{SO}_4^{2-}(aq) \rightarrow \text{BaSO}_4(s)$. Atoms: 1 Ba, 1 S, 4 O both sides ✓. Charge: left (+2) + (-2) = 0; right 0 (neutral solid) ✓.
principles.example: Net ionic: $\text{H}^+(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. Atoms: 2 H, 1 O both sides ✓. Charge: left (+1) + (-1) = 0; right 0 ✓. Always verify!

image.alt: Verification checklist for net ionic equations
image.caption: Verify net ionic: atoms balanced? charge balanced? simplest coefficients?

principles.statement: Reduce coefficients to lowest whole-number ratio in net ionic equations, just like balancing regular equations.
principles.application: $2\text{H}^+(aq) + 2\text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$ simplifies to $\text{H}^+(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. Divide all coefficients by 2.

image.alt: Example of simplifying net ionic equation coefficients
image.caption: Simplify: reduce coefficients to lowest whole-number ratio.

classifications.value: Show formation of insoluble precipitate from aqueous ions
characteristics: Same net ionic for all reactions forming same precipitate
classifications.behavior: Net ionic shows only the ions forming the precipitate. All soluble salt combinations that form the same precipitate have identical net ionic equations.

examples.explanation: Molecular: $\text{AgNO}_3(aq) + \text{NaCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s) + \text{NaNO}_3(aq)$. Complete ionic: $\text{Ag}^+ + \text{NO}_3^- + \text{Na}^+ + \text{Cl}^- \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s) + \text{Na}^+ + \text{NO}_3^-$. Net ionic: $\text{Ag}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s)$.

image.alt: Net ionic equation for silver chloride precipitation
image.caption: Precipitation net ionic: $\text{Ag}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s)$.

examples.explanation: Net ionic: $\text{Ba}^{2+}(aq) + \text{SO}_4^{2-}(aq) \rightarrow \text{BaSO}_4(s)$. Same net ionic regardless of which soluble barium salt and sulfate salt used.

image.alt: Net ionic equation for barium sulfate precipitation
image.caption: Precipitation net ionic: $\text{Ba}^{2+}(aq) + \text{SO}_4^{2-}(aq) \rightarrow \text{BaSO}_4(s)$.

classifications.value: Show formation of water from $\text{H}^+$ and $\text{OH}^-$ ions
characteristics: Same net ionic for all strong acid-strong base reactions
classifications.behavior: All strong acid + strong base neutralizations have the same net ionic equation. Weak acids/bases have different net ionics because they don't fully dissociate.

examples.explanation: $\text{HCl}(aq) + \text{NaOH}(aq) \rightarrow \text{NaCl}(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. Net ionic: $\text{H}^+(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. Universal for strong acid-strong base.

image.alt: Net ionic equation for strong acid-strong base neutralization
image.caption: Neutralization net ionic: $\text{H}^+(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$.

examples.explanation: $\text{HC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2^-(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. Weak acid kept as formula. Different from strong acid neutralization.

image.alt: Net ionic for weak acid-strong base neutralization
image.caption: Weak acid neutralization: weak acid kept as formula in net ionic.

classifications.value: Show metal oxidation and ion reduction
characteristics: Metal + aqueous ion → different metal ion + different metal
classifications.behavior: Net ionic shows electron transfer. Anion is spectator. Metal is oxidized (loses e⁻), aqueous ion is reduced (gains e⁻).

examples.explanation: Molecular: $\text{Zn}(s) + \text{CuSO}_4(aq) \rightarrow \text{ZnSO}_4(aq) + \text{Cu}(s)$. Net ionic: $\text{Zn}(s) + \text{Cu}^{2+}(aq) \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}(aq) + \text{Cu}(s)$. Spectator: $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$.

image.alt: Net ionic equation for zinc-copper displacement
image.caption: Single displacement net ionic: $\text{Zn}(s) + \text{Cu}^{2+}(aq) \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}(aq) + \text{Cu}(s)$.

examples.explanation: Net ionic: $\text{Mg}(s) + 2\text{H}^+(aq) \rightarrow \text{Mg}^{2+}(aq) + \text{H}_2(g)$. Spectator: anion from acid (e.g., $\text{Cl}^-$, $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$).

image.alt: Net ionic for magnesium displacing hydrogen from acid
image.caption: Metal + acid net ionic: $\text{Mg}(s) + 2\text{H}^+(aq) \rightarrow \text{Mg}^{2+}(aq) + \text{H}_2(g)$.

classifications.value: Show formation of gas from aqueous ions
characteristics: Often involves carbonates, sulfites, or sulfides + acid
classifications.behavior: Net ionic shows ions forming unstable intermediate that decomposes to gas. Gas escapes, driving reaction to completion.

examples.explanation: Molecular: $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3(aq) + 2\text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow 2\text{NaCl}(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) + \text{CO}_2(g)$. Net ionic: $\text{CO}_3^{2-}(aq) + 2\text{H}^+(aq) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) + \text{CO}_2(g)$. Spectators: $\text{Na}^+$, $\text{Cl}^-$.

image.alt: Net ionic for carbonate-acid gas formation
image.caption: Gas-forming net ionic: $\text{CO}_3^{2-}(aq) + 2\text{H}^+(aq) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) + \text{CO}_2(g)$.

examples.explanation: Net ionic: $\text{S}^{2-}(aq) + 2\text{H}^+(aq) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{S}(g)$. Rotten egg smell. Toxic gas—use in fume hood.

image.alt: Net ionic for sulfide-acid gas formation
image.caption: Gas-forming net ionic: $\text{S}^{2-}(aq) + 2\text{H}^+(aq) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{S}(g)$.

components.equation: $\text{Net Ionic Steps: } \begin{cases} \text{1. Write balanced molecular equation} \\ \text{2. Write complete ionic equation (dissociate strong electrolytes)} \\ \text{3. Cancel spectator ions (identical on both sides)} \\ \text{4. Write net ionic equation (remaining species)} \\ \text{5. Verify atom and charge balance} \end{cases}$
components.interpretation: Follow steps in order. Cancel only identical ions (same formula, charge, state, coefficient). Verify final net ionic is balanced.

worked_examples.title: Writing Net Ionic Equation for Precipitation
worked_examples.problem: Write the net ionic equation for the reaction between calcium chloride and sodium carbonate.

step_by_step.calculation: $\text{CaCl}_2(aq) + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3(aq) \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3(s) + 2\text{NaCl}(aq)$. $\text{CaCO}_3$ is insoluble (carbonate exception).

step_by_step.explanation: Write complete ionic equation (dissociate soluble compounds).
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Ca}^{2+}(aq) + 2\text{Cl}^-(aq) + 2\text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{CO}_3^{2-}(aq) \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3(s) + 2\text{Na}^+(aq) + 2\text{Cl}^-(aq)$
Spectators: $\text{Na}^+(aq)$ and $\text{Cl}^-(aq)$ (appear on both sides). Cancel them.

step_by_step.explanation: Write net ionic equation with remaining species.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Ca}^{2+}(aq) + \text{CO}_3^{2-}(aq) \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3(s)$
Atoms: 1 Ca, 1 C, 3 O both sides ✓. Charge: left (+2) + (-2) = 0; right 0 (neutral solid) ✓. Balanced.

worked_examples.final_answer: Net ionic: $\text{Ca}^{2+}(aq) + \text{CO}_3^{2-}(aq) \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3(s)$. Spectator ions ($\text{Na}^+$, $\text{Cl}^-$) removed. Shows actual chemical change: calcium ions combining with carbonate ions to form precipitate.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Writing net ionic equation for precipitation reaction with spectator ion cancellation

worked_examples.title: Writing Net Ionic Equation for Neutralization
worked_examples.problem: Write the net ionic equation for the reaction between nitric acid and barium hydroxide.

step_by_step.calculation: $2\text{HNO}_3(aq) + \text{Ba}(\text{OH})_2(aq) \rightarrow \text{Ba}(\text{NO}_3)_2(aq) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. Acid + base → salt + water.
$2\text{H}^+(aq) + 2\text{NO}_3^-(aq) + \text{Ba}^{2+}(aq) + 2\text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{Ba}^{2+}(aq) + 2\text{NO}_3^-(aq) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$
Spectators: $\text{Ba}^{2+}(aq)$ and $\text{NO}_3^-(aq)$ (appear on both sides). Cancel them.
$2\text{H}^+(aq) + 2\text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$
Simplify: divide all by 2 → $\text{H}^+(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. Verify: 2 H, 1 O both sides ✓. Charge: (+1) + (-1) = 0 ✓.

worked_examples.final_answer: Net ionic: $\text{H}^+(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. This is the net ionic for ALL strong acid-strong base neutralizations. Spectator ions ($\text{Ba}^{2+}$, $\text{NO}_3^-$) are the salt ions that remain in solution.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Writing net ionic equation for neutralization; recognizing universal net ionic for strong acid-strong base reactions

sections.section_title: Special Cases in Net Ionic Equations
sections.introduction: Some reactions require special handling when writing net ionic equations. Weak electrolytes, gases, and reactions with no net change all have unique considerations that must be understood for accurate net ionic equation writing.

key_concept.definition: Special cases in net ionic equations include: weak electrolytes (keep as formulas), gases (keep as formulas), reactions with no net change (all spectators = no reaction), and reactions where all species are aqueous with no precipitate/gas/water.
key_concept.context: These special cases often confuse students. Weak acids/bases don't dissociate significantly. Gases escape solution. If all ions are spectators, no reaction occurs. Understanding these exceptions is essential for accurate net ionic equations.

image.alt: Chart showing special cases in net ionic equations
image.caption: Special cases: weak electrolytes, gases, no reaction, all aqueous.

classifications.value: Weak acids and bases remain as formulas in net ionic equations
characteristics: Keep as molecular formulas in net ionic
classifications.behavior: Weak electrolytes appear as formulas in both complete and net ionic equations. They react but don't dissociate. Net ionic shows the molecular species reacting.

examples.explanation: $\text{HC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2^-(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. Weak acid kept as formula. Different from strong acid neutralization.

image.alt: Net ionic equation with weak acid
image.caption: Weak acid net ionic: weak acid kept as formula, not dissociated.

examples.explanation: $\text{NH}_3(aq) + \text{H}^+(aq) \rightarrow \text{NH}_4^+(aq)$. Ammonia kept as formula. Reacts with $\text{H}^+$ to form ammonium ion.

image.alt: Net ionic equation with ammonia
image.caption: Weak base net ionic: $\text{NH}_3$ kept as formula.

classifications.value: Gases appear as molecular formulas with $(g)$ state in net ionic equations
characteristics: Keep as molecular formula with $(g)$ state
classifications.behavior: Gases don't dissociate into ions. Write as molecular formulas with $(g)$ state. Gas formation is driving force for reaction.

examples.explanation: $\text{CO}_3^{2-}(aq) + 2\text{H}^+(aq) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) + \text{CO}_2(g)$. $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$ intermediate decomposes immediately to $\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2$.

image.alt: Net ionic showing CO2 gas formation
image.caption: Gas-forming net ionic: $\text{CO}_2(g)$ escapes, drives reaction.

examples.explanation: $\text{Zn}(s) + 2\text{H}^+(aq) \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}(aq) + \text{H}_2(g)$. Hydrogen gas escapes. Single displacement with gas formation.

image.alt: Net ionic showing H2 gas formation
image.caption: Gas-forming net ionic: $\text{H}_2(g)$ escapes solution.

classifications.value: If all ions are spectators, no reaction occurs; write 'no reaction'
characteristics: No precipitate, gas, or water formed
Net ionic would have no remaining species
classifications.behavior: If complete ionic equation shows all ions are spectators (identical on both sides), no chemical change occurs. Write 'no reaction' instead of trying to write meaningless net ionic.

examples.explanation: $\text{NaNO}_3(aq) + \text{KCl}(aq) \rightarrow$? Products: $\text{NaCl}(aq) + \text{KNO}_3(aq)$. All soluble (Group 1 + nitrates). Complete ionic shows all spectators. Answer: NO REACTION.

image.alt: Example of no reaction case
image.caption: No reaction: all ions are spectators, no chemical change occurs.

examples.explanation: $\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4(aq) + \text{NaNO}_3(aq) \rightarrow$? All possible products soluble. No precipitate/gas/water. Answer: NO REACTION. Solubility rules predict this.

image.alt: Example of no driving force for reaction
image.caption: No driving force: no precipitate, gas, or water → no reaction.

classifications.value: Solids and liquids remain as formulas in net ionic equations
characteristics: Do not dissociate; keep as formulas
classifications.behavior: Solids and liquids don't dissociate into ions. Write as complete formulas with $(s)$ or $(l)$ state. They may react but remain as formulas in net ionic.

examples.explanation: $\text{Zn}(s) + \text{Cu}^{2+}(aq) \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}(aq) + \text{Cu}(s)$. Both metals remain as formulas with $(s)$ state. Only aqueous ions dissociate.

image.alt: Net ionic with solid metal reactant
image.caption: Solid reactants: keep as formulas with $(s)$ state.

examples.explanation: $\text{H}^+(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. Water is liquid product, kept as formula. Not dissociated.

image.alt: Net ionic with water as liquid product
image.caption: Liquid products: keep as formulas with $(l)$ state.

principles.statement: Memorize common weak acids and bases to know when to keep as formulas in net ionic equations.
principles.application: Weak acids: $\text{HC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2$ (acetic), $\text{HF}$, $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$, $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$, $\text{HNO}_2$. Weak bases: $\text{NH}_3$, organic amines. All others (strong acids/bases) dissociate.

image.alt: List of common weak acids and bases
image.caption: Weak electrolytes: keep as formulas in net ionic equations.

principles.statement: If complete ionic equation shows all ions are spectators (identical on both sides), write 'no reaction' instead of net ionic.
principles.application: $\text{NaCl}(aq) + \text{KNO}_3(aq) \rightarrow$ complete ionic: $\text{Na}^+ + \text{Cl}^- + \text{K}^+ + \text{NO}_3^- \rightarrow \text{Na}^+ + \text{Cl}^- + \text{K}^+ + \text{NO}_3^-$. All spectators → NO REACTION.
principles.example: Use solubility rules to predict: if both possible products are soluble, no reaction occurs. Save time by checking solubility before writing complete ionic.

image.alt: Flowchart for determining no reaction cases
image.caption: No reaction: all spectators → no chemical change.

components.equation: $\text{Special Cases: } \begin{cases} \text{Weak electrolytes} \rightarrow \text{keep as formulas} \\ \text{Gases } (g) \rightarrow \text{keep as formulas} \\ \text{Solids } (s), \text{liquids } (l) \rightarrow \text{keep as formulas} \\ \text{All spectators} \rightarrow \text{no reaction} \end{cases}$
components.interpretation: Only strong electrolytes (soluble ionic, strong acids/bases) dissociate. Everything else stays as formulas. All spectators = no reaction.

worked_examples.title: Net Ionic with Weak Acid
worked_examples.problem: Write the net ionic equation for the reaction between acetic acid ($\text{HC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2$) and sodium hydroxide.

step_by_step.calculation: $\text{HC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2(aq) + \text{NaOH}(aq) \rightarrow \text{NaC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$

step_by_step.explanation: Write complete ionic equation (keep weak acid as formula).
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{HC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2(aq) + \text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{C}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2^-(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. Weak acid NOT dissociated.
Spectator: $\text{Na}^+(aq)$ (appears on both sides). Cancel it.
$\text{HC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2^-(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$
Atoms: 2 C, 4 H, 3 O both sides ✓. Charge: left 0 + (-1) = -1; right (-1) + 0 = -1 ✓. Balanced.

worked_examples.final_answer: Net ionic: $\text{HC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2^-(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. Weak acid kept as formula (doesn't dissociate). Different from strong acid neutralization ($\text{H}^+ + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Writing net ionic equation with weak acid (keep weak acid as formula, not dissociated)

worked_examples.title: Determining No Reaction Case
worked_examples.problem: Determine if a reaction occurs between $\text{Li}_2\text{SO}_4(aq)$ and $\text{NaNO}_3(aq)$. If yes, write net ionic. If no, write 'no reaction'.

step_by_step.explanation: Predict possible products by exchanging ions.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Li}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{NaNO}_3 \rightarrow \text{LiNO}_3 + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$ (exchange $\text{Li}^+$ with $\text{Na}^+$)

step_by_step.explanation: Check solubility of products using solubility rules.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{LiNO}_3$: soluble (Group 1 + nitrate). $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$: soluble (Group 1). Both products soluble.
No precipitate (both soluble), no gas, no water formed. No driving force for reaction.

step_by_step.explanation: Write complete ionic to verify all spectators.
step_by_step.calculation: $2\text{Li}^+ + \text{SO}_4^{2-} + 2\text{Na}^+ + \text{NO}_3^- \rightarrow 2\text{Li}^+ + \text{NO}_3^- + 2\text{Na}^+ + \text{SO}_4^{2-}$. All ions appear on both sides = all spectators.
All ions are spectators → no chemical change → NO REACTION

worked_examples.final_answer: NO REACTION. Both possible products ($\text{LiNO}_3$ and $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$) are soluble (Group 1 salts). All ions are spectators. No precipitate, gas, or water forms. No driving force for reaction. Use solubility rules to predict this before writing equations.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Determining no reaction case using solubility rules and spectator ion analysis

sections.section_title: Common Errors in Net Ionic Equations
sections.introduction: Students often make predictable errors when writing net ionic equations. Recognizing these pitfalls and learning correction strategies builds accuracy and confidence in net ionic equation writing skills.

key_concept.definition: Common net ionic errors include: dissociating weak electrolytes, not canceling all spectators, dissociating solids/liquids/gases, writing net ionic for no reaction cases, and not verifying charge balance.
key_concept.context: A verification checklist prevents errors: strong electrolytes dissociated? weak electrolytes kept as formulas? all spectators canceled? charge balanced? no reaction cases identified? Systematic approach minimizes mistakes.

image.alt: Checklist for verifying net ionic equations
image.caption: Error prevention: check dissociation, spectators, charge balance, no reaction cases.

classifications.value: Incorrectly writing weak acids/bases as ions instead of formulas
characteristics: Treating weak electrolytes like strong electrolytes
classifications.behavior: Results in incorrect net ionic equations. Weak acids/bases don't dissociate significantly. Keep as molecular formulas in complete and net ionic equations.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: $\text{H}^+(aq) + \text{C}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2^-(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2^-(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. Correct: $\text{HC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2^-(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. Keep weak acid as formula.

image.alt: Comparison of incorrect vs. correct weak acid treatment
image.caption: Net ionic error: weak acids stay as formulas, don't dissociate.

classifications.value: Leaving spectator ions in net ionic equation
characteristics: Net ionic has ions that should be canceled
classifications.behavior: Results in incomplete net ionic equations. Must cancel ALL identical ions on both sides. Compare carefully: same formula, charge, state, coefficient.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: $\text{Ag}^+(aq) + \text{NO}_3^-(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s)$. Correct: $\text{Ag}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s)$. $\text{NO}_3^-$ is spectator, must cancel.

image.alt: Example of incomplete spectator cancellation
image.caption: Net ionic error: cancel ALL spectators, not just some.

classifications.value: Incorrectly writing solids, liquids, or gases as ions
classifications.behavior: Results in chemically impossible equations. Solids, liquids, gases don't dissociate. Keep as complete formulas with state symbols.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: $\text{Ag}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{Ag}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq)$. Correct: $\text{Ag}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s)$. Precipitate stays as formula with $(s)$ state.

image.alt: Example of incorrectly dissociating solid precipitate
image.caption: Net ionic error: solids/liquids/gases don't dissociate; keep as formulas.

classifications.type: Writing Net Ionic for No Reaction
classifications.value: Attempting to write net ionic when no reaction occurs
characteristics: Net ionic would have no remaining species
classifications.behavior: Results in meaningless equations. If all ions cancel, no chemical change occurs. Write 'no reaction' instead of trying to force a net ionic.

examples.process: Error: net ionic for no reaction case
examples.explanation: Incorrect: $\text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq)$ (for $\text{NaCl} + \text{KNO}_3$). Correct: NO REACTION. All spectators, no chemical change.

image.alt: Example of attempting net ionic for no reaction case
image.caption: Net ionic error: if all spectators → no reaction, don't write net ionic.

classifications.value: Writing net ionic without checking charge balance
characteristics: Total charge left ≠ total charge right
classifications.behavior: Results in impossible equations. Net ionic must balance both atoms AND charge. Always verify: sum charges on left = sum charges on right.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: $\text{Fe}^{2+}(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{FeCl}_2(s)$. Left charge: (+2) + (-1) = +1; Right charge: 0. Not balanced! Correct: $\text{Fe}^{2+}(aq) + 2\text{Cl}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{FeCl}_2(s)$. Left: (+2) + 2(-1) = 0 = Right ✓.

image.alt: Example of charge imbalance in net ionic equation
image.caption: Net ionic error: always verify charge balance (left charge = right charge).

principles.statement: Always verify net ionic equations by checking: (1) Strong electrolytes dissociated correctly, (2) Weak electrolytes kept as formulas, (3) Solids/liquids/gases kept as formulas, (4) All spectators canceled, (5) Atoms balanced, (6) Charge balanced, (7) No reaction cases identified.
principles.application: Prevents common errors; systematic verification builds accuracy habit. Use checklist for every net ionic equation.
principles.example: Net ionic: $\text{Ag}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s)$. Check: Strong electrolytes dissociated ($\text{AgNO}_3$, $\text{NaCl}$) ✓; No weak electrolytes ✓; Solid kept as formula ✓; Spectators ($\text{Na}^+$, $\text{NO}_3^-$) canceled ✓; Atoms balanced (1 Ag, 1 Cl) ✓; Charge balanced (0 = 0) ✓. Verified.

image.alt: Checklist for verifying net ionic equations
image.caption: Verification: dissociation, formulas, spectators, atoms, charge, no reaction.

principles.statement: State symbols determine whether to dissociate: $(aq)$ ionic = dissociate; $(s)$, $(l)$, $(g)$ = keep as formulas; $(aq)$ weak electrolyte = keep as formula.
principles.application: $\text{NaCl}(aq)$ → dissociate. $\text{AgCl}(s)$ → keep as formula. $\text{HC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2(aq)$ → keep as formula (weak). $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$ → keep as formula. State symbols are your guide.
principles.example: Complete ionic: $\text{H}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq) + \text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$ kept as formula (liquid), not dissociated.

image.alt: Chart showing how state symbols guide dissociation
image.caption: State symbols: $(aq)$ ionic = dissociate; $(s)$, $(l)$, $(g)$, weak = keep as formula.

worked_examples.title: Error Identification and Correction
worked_examples.problem: Identify the error in each net ionic attempt and provide the correct solution: (a) $\text{HC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{H}^+(aq) + \text{C}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2^-(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$ (dissociated weak acid), (b) $\text{Ag}^+(aq) + \text{NO}_3^-(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s)$ (incomplete cancellation), (c) $\text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq)$ (no reaction case).

step_by_step.calculation: Error: weak acid $\text{HC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2$ dissociated. Correct: $\text{HC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2^-(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. Keep weak acid as formula.
Error: $\text{NO}_3^-$ spectator not canceled. Correct: $\text{Ag}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s)$. Cancel ALL spectators.
Error: wrote net ionic when all ions are spectators. Correct: NO REACTION. All ions cancel → no chemical change.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Error: dissociated weak acid. Correction: $\text{HC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2^-(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. (b) Error: incomplete spectator cancellation. Correction: $\text{Ag}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s)$. (c) Error: net ionic for no reaction. Correction: NO REACTION. Verification checklist prevents these errors.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Identifying and correcting common net ionic equation errors

sections.introduction: Net ionic equation mastery requires active practice with spectator ion identification, complete ionic writing, and special case recognition. Work through these problems to build confidence and accuracy in writing net ionic equations.

key_concept.definition: Practice problems cover: writing complete ionic equations, identifying spectator ions, writing net ionic equations, recognizing special cases (weak electrolytes, gases, no reaction), and verifying balance.
key_concept.context: Active recall and immediate feedback reinforce dissociation rules and spectator identification; focus on systematic approach (molecular → complete ionic → net ionic), weak electrolyte recognition, and verification steps.

image.alt: Practice strategy flowchart for net ionic equations
image.caption: Practice strategy: molecular → complete ionic → cancel spectators → net ionic → verify.

worked_examples.title: Comprehensive Net Ionic Practice Set
worked_examples.problem: Write net ionic equations (write 'no reaction' if none): (a) $\text{BaCl}_2(aq) + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4(aq) \rightarrow$, (b) $\text{HNO}_3(aq) + \text{KOH}(aq) \rightarrow$, (c) $\text{NaNO}_3(aq) + \text{KCl}(aq) \rightarrow$, (d) $\text{Zn}(s) + \text{CuSO}_4(aq) \rightarrow$, (e) $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3(aq) + \text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow$, (f) $\text{HC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2(aq) + \text{NaOH}(aq) \rightarrow$.

step_by_step.calculation: Molecular: $\text{BaCl}_2(aq) + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4(aq) \rightarrow \text{BaSO}_4(s) + 2\text{NaCl}(aq)$. Complete ionic: $\text{Ba}^{2+} + 2\text{Cl}^- + 2\text{Na}^+ + \text{SO}_4^{2-} \rightarrow \text{BaSO}_4(s) + 2\text{Na}^+ + 2\text{Cl}^-$. Spectators: $\text{Na}^+$, $\text{Cl}^-$. Net ionic: $\text{Ba}^{2+}(aq) + \text{SO}_4^{2-}(aq) \rightarrow \text{BaSO}_4(s)$.

Molecular: $\text{HNO}_3(aq) + \text{KOH}(aq) \rightarrow \text{KNO}_3(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. Strong acid + strong base. Net ionic: $\text{H}^+(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$.
Products: $\text{NaCl} + \text{KNO}_3$. Both soluble (Group 1 + nitrates). All spectators. Net ionic: NO REACTION.

Molecular: $\text{Zn}(s) + \text{CuSO}_4(aq) \rightarrow \text{ZnSO}_4(aq) + \text{Cu}(s)$. Complete ionic: $\text{Zn}(s) + \text{Cu}^{2+} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} + \text{Cu}(s)$. Spectator: $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$. Net ionic: $\text{Zn}(s) + \text{Cu}^{2+}(aq) \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}(aq) + \text{Cu}(s)$.

Molecular: $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3(aq) + 2\text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow 2\text{NaCl}(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) + \text{CO}_2(g)$. Gas-forming. Net ionic: $\text{CO}_3^{2-}(aq) + 2\text{H}^+(aq) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) + \text{CO}_2(g)$.
Weak acid + strong base. Keep weak acid as formula. Net ionic: $\text{HC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2^-(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) $\text{Ba}^{2+}(aq) + \text{SO}_4^{2-}(aq) \rightarrow \text{BaSO}_4(s)$ (precipitation). (b) $\text{H}^+(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$ (neutralization). (c) NO REACTION (all spectators). (d) $\text{Zn}(s) + \text{Cu}^{2+}(aq) \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}(aq) + \text{Cu}(s)$ (single displacement). (e) $\text{CO}_3^{2-}(aq) + 2\text{H}^+(aq) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) + \text{CO}_2(g)$ (gas-forming). (f) $\text{HC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2^-(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$ (weak acid neutralization). Key: identify type, write complete ionic, cancel spectators, verify.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Comprehensive net ionic equation practice across all reaction types with special cases

worked_examples.title: Advanced Net Ionic Challenge

step_by_step.calculation: Weak base + strong acid. $\text{NH}_3$ kept as formula. Molecular: $\text{NH}_3(aq) + \text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{Cl}(aq)$. Net ionic: $\text{NH}_3(aq) + \text{H}^+(aq) \rightarrow \text{NH}_4^+(aq)$.

Solid carbonate + acid. $\text{CaCO}_3(s)$ kept as formula (solid). Molecular: $\text{CaCO}_3(s) + 2\text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{CaCl}_2(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) + \text{CO}_2(g)$. Net ionic: $\text{CaCO}_3(s) + 2\text{H}^+(aq) \rightarrow \text{Ca}^{2+}(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) + \text{CO}_2(g)$.

Solid metal + acid. $\text{Fe}(s)$ kept as formula. Molecular: $\text{Fe}(s) + 2\text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{FeCl}_2(aq) + \text{H}_2(g)$. Net ionic: $\text{Fe}(s) + 2\text{H}^+(aq) \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+}(aq) + \text{H}_2(g)$.

Precipitation. $\text{AgBr}$ insoluble (Ag exception). Molecular: $\text{AgNO}_3(aq) + \text{NaBr}(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgBr}(s) + \text{NaNO}_3(aq)$. Net ionic: $\text{Ag}^+(aq) + \text{Br}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgBr}(s)$.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) $\text{NH}_3(aq) + \text{H}^+(aq) \rightarrow \text{NH}_4^+(aq)$ (weak base + acid). (b) $\text{CaCO}_3(s) + 2\text{H}^+(aq) \rightarrow \text{Ca}^{2+}(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) + \text{CO}_2(g)$ (solid carbonate + acid). (c) $\text{Fe}(s) + 2\text{H}^+(aq) \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+}(aq) + \text{H}_2(g)$ (metal + acid). (d) $\text{Ag}^+(aq) + \text{Br}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgBr}(s)$ (precipitation). Key: solids/liquids/gases/weak electrolytes kept as formulas; only aqueous strong electrolytes dissociate.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Advanced net ionic practice with solids, weak bases, and gas-forming reactions

summary: Net ionic equations show only species that actually participate in chemical reactions by removing spectator ions (ions unchanged on both sides). Writing process: (1) Write balanced molecular equation, (2) Write complete ionic equation (dissociate strong electrolytes: soluble ionic compounds, strong acids/bases; keep weak electrolytes, solids, liquids, gases as formulas), (3) Cancel spectator ions (identical ions on both sides), (4) Write net ionic with remaining species, (5) Verify atom and charge balance. Spectator ions: appear unchanged on both sides of complete ionic equation; removed to show actual chemical change. Special cases: weak acids/bases ($\text{HC}_2\text{H}_3\text{O}_2$, $\text{NH}_3$) kept as formulas; solids/liquids/gases kept as formulas; if all ions are spectators → NO REACTION. Net ionic types: precipitation ($\text{Ag}^+ + \text{Cl}^- \rightarrow \text{AgCl}$), neutralization ($\text{H}^+ + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$ for strong acid-strong base), single displacement ($\text{Zn} + \text{Cu}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + \text{Cu}$), gas-forming ($\text{CO}_3^{2-} + 2\text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2$). Common errors: dissociating weak electrolytes, not canceling all spectators, dissociating solids/liquids/gases, writing net ionic for no reaction cases, not verifying charge balance. Verification checklist: strong electrolytes dissociated? weak electrolytes kept as formulas? all spectators canceled? atoms balanced? charge balanced? no reaction identified? Mastery enables clear understanding of actual chemical changes occurring in aqueous reactions.
